title: Coffee on Polyester
stainChemistry: Coffee contains tannins (polyphenolic compounds from roasted beans) that bond to polyester through hydrogen bonding and pi-stacking interactions. Fresh coffee is water-soluble. Aged stains oxidize and darken — the brown color intensifies as tannin molecules cross-link. Melanoidins from the roasting process add additional brown pigment that is highly resistant to removal.
whyThisWorks: An acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) breaks the chromophore bonds — the colored structures — in the tannin molecules on polyester. Hydrogen peroxide specifically targets the double bonds that make oxidized tannin brown. The key is avoiding heat and alkaline products, both of which permanently darken tannin stains.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester rule: surface-bound, no high heat
homeSolutions.instruction: Polyester's low absorbency works in your favor — coffee sits on the fiber rather than soaking in. But polyester is heat-sensitive: hot coffee on polyester cools quickly (good for your counter-attack), but hot water later will shrink or melt the fiber. Cold water at every step, no tumble-dry until the stain is fully out.

On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn + 3% peroxide solution; blot with a white cloth after 60 seconds. If it holds color, proceed. Tannin rule: no ammonia, baking soda, or OxiClean on untreated tannin — all lock brown into the fiber permanently.

Turn the fabric inside out and run cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. On polyester, cold-water flush alone often lifts most of a fresh coffee spill. Skip hot water — it drives tannin into the fiber.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the stain outside-in. Let sit 5–10 minutes, blot with a clean damp cloth. Most polyester coffee stains lift in one cycle — the stain is surface-bound. Repeat once if a shadow remains.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dab onto the spot with a white cloth. Neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps residual tannin in its lighter form. Blot dry with a fresh cloth.

Launder on cold with your normal detergent. Air-dry; do NOT put in the dryer until you've inspected in daylight. Polyester fuses pigment at dryer heat — any remaining shadow will become permanent on the first hot cycle.

Inspect in natural light. If a pale tan shadow remains, repeat steps 4–6 once more. Two rounds is the home ceiling. A brown halo (rather than tan) after two rounds means the tannin has polymerized — escalate.

If the stain has been through a hot dryer, sat for days, or survived two home rounds, take it in. Tell them: "Coffee on polyester, age [fresh/days/heat-set], treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar rinse. No alkaline, no hot water." Polyester is one of the easier fibers for pros because the stain is surface-bound — they'll finish it with 6% peroxide + a tannin formula fast.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If coffee stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is coffee on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
